region_of_origin: Mediterranean (Apium graveolens); widely cultivated and imported into Singapore
food_subgroup: Stalk vegetable, raw; very low calorie (~16 kcal/100g); naturally occurring sodium

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 100g reference portion of raw celery stalks with leaves trimmed, as sold in Singapore supermarkets, providing approximately 16 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Celery, Raw — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 169988)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match to USDA SR Legacy entry for raw celery (FDC ID 169988). All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from this entry. Confidence: very high.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.; corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database entry for celery
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~15 for raw celery is consistent with published values in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Available carbohydrate per 100g = 3.0g total carbohydrate − 1.6g dietary fibre = 1.4g. GL = (15 × 1.4) ÷ 100 × 100 = 0.21, rounded to 0.2 per 100g reference serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Naturally occurring sodium 80 mg/100g with a favourable potassium:sodium ratio of 3.25:1 (260 mg K : 80 mg Na)
hypertension.guidance: Raw celery is suitable for most hypertensive patients; however, patients on very-low-sodium diets (< 200 mg/day, e.g., severe CKD or refractory CHF) should note that 100g celery contributes approximately 40% of that daily limit from naturally occurring sodium alone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~15 (very low), GL ~0.2 per 100g; available carbohydrate only 1.4g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw celery has essentially no glycaemic impact at any realistic serving size and is an excellent low-energy snack (16 kcal/100g) to displace higher-calorie, higher-GI between-meal foods in the dietary management of Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero trans fat, zero cholesterol; high potassium (260 mg/100g); very low energy density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw celery is cardioprotective in dietary context: its negligible saturated fat and cholesterol content, combined with a high potassium:sodium ratio, make it a suitable unrestricted vegetable for cardiovascular disease patients following a heart-healthy eating pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low calorie density supports weight management relevant to joint load reduction
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Celery's very low calorie density (16 kcal/100g) supports weight management, which reduces mechanical joint load; apigenin (flavone) in celery has demonstrated anti-inflammatory activity in cell studies, though clinical dose at food amounts is negligible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Celery (Raw) is a crisp, pale-green stalk vegetable (Apium graveolens) consumed raw or cooked, widely available in Singapore supermarkets as an imported produce item. Per 100g raw: 16 kcal — one of the lowest calorie densities of any food — 0.7g protein, 0.2g fat (0g saturated), 3.0g carbohydrate (1.3g sugar, 1.6g dietary fibre), 80mg naturally occurring sodium, and 260mg potassium. GI ~15 (very low), GL ~0.2 per 100g. NEGATIVE CALORIE MYTH: celery is NOT negative calorie — its thermic effect of feeding (TEF) is approximately 1.1 kcal, yielding a net ~14.9 kcal per 100g. It is genuinely very low calorie, not negative. The naturally occurring sodium (80mg/100g) is clinically noteworthy only for patients on very-low-sodium diets (< 200mg/day), where 100g celery represents ~40% of the daily limit. Vitamin K content is low (~29.3 mcg/100g) and is not a warfarin interaction concern at typical portions. Celery is used raw as a snack vegetable, as a stir-fry aromatic (西芹 in Chinese cuisine), and as a stock or broth ingredient. It is vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Source: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169988. Confidence: very high.

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169988 (raw celery), an approved primary source. GI value of ~15 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) are verified direct matches. No estimation or analogy required for core nutrients.
notes: Saturated fat is confirmed zero (0.0g) per USDA SR Legacy FDC 169988 — celery contains negligible saturated fatty acids. Trans fat is confirmed zero (0.0g) per USDA SR Legacy — celery is a raw plant food with no industrial trans fats. Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0g) — plant food, no dietary cholesterol. Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero (0.0g) — plant food, no cobalamin. Vitamin D is confirmed zero (0.0g) per USDA SR Legacy — raw celery contains no meaningful vitamin D. Monounsaturated fat (0.04g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.08g) are present in trace amounts per USDA SR Legacy. Water content (95.4g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 169988. Vitamin K content (~29.3 mcg/100g) is noted in the LLM description for clinical context but is not included as a separate per_100g field in this template version. NEGATIVE CALORIE MYTH documented: celery TEF ~1.1 kcal, net ~14.9 kcal per 100g — it is very low calorie, not negative calorie. Natural sodium (80mg/100g) is clinically relevant only for patients on very-low-sodium diets (< 200mg/day). No -1 sentinel values used; all fields populated from USDA SR Legacy or set to confirmed zero for plant-food nutrients. Aliases consistency self-check: all string values in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array — english: [Celery raw, Celery stalks raw, Raw celery, Celery stalk, Western celery raw] ✓; chinese: [西芹 raw, 西芹（生）, 芹菜（生）] ✓; malay: [Saderi Mentah, Saderi (Mentah)] ✓; tamil: [செலரி (பச்சை)] ✓; hindi: [अजवाइन की पत्ती (कच्ची)] ✓; romanized: [Qín cài (shēng), Apium graveolens raw] ✓.
